name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَـٰحِهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٨٥)
```

```english
And to [the people of] Midian [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. There has certainly come to you a manifest proof from your Lord. Observe fully the measure and the balance, and do not cheat the people of their goods, and do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration. That is better for you, if you are faithful. (85)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَقْعُدُوا۟ بِكُلِّ صِرَٰطٍ تُوعِدُونَ وَتَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ وَتَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ كُنتُمْ قَلِيلًا فَكَثَّرَكُمْ ۖ وَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٨٦)
```

```english
And do not lie in wait on every road to threaten and bar those who have faith in Him from the way of Allah, seeking to make it crooked. Remember when you were few, and He multiplied you, and observe how was the fate of the agents of corruption. (86)
```

```arabic
وَإِن كَانَ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنكُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦ وَطَآئِفَةٌ لَّمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ فَٱصْبِرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ بَيْنَنَا ۚ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (٨٧)
```

```english
If a group of you have believed in what I have been sent with, and a group have not believed, be patient until Allah judges between us, and He is the best of judges.’ (87)
```

```arabic
۞ قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَكَ مِن قَرْيَتِنَآ أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِى مِلَّتِنَا ۚ قَالَ أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَـٰرِهِينَ (٨٨)
```

```english
The elite of his people who were arrogant said, ‘O Shu'ayb, we will surely expel you and the faithful who are with you from our town, or else you shall revert to our creed.’ He said, ‘What! Even if we should be unwilling?! (88)
```

```arabic
قَدِ ٱفْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا إِنْ عُدْنَا فِى مِلَّتِكُم بَعْدَ إِذْ نَجَّىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ مِنْهَا ۚ وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَآ أَن نَّعُودَ فِيهَآ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا ۚ وَسِعَ رَبُّنَا كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا ۚ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا ٱفْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰتِحِينَ (٨٩)
```

```english
We would be fabricating a lie against Allah should we revert to your creed after Allah had delivered us from it. It does not behoove us to return to it, unless Allah, our Lord, should wish so. Our Lord embraces all things in [His] knowledge. In Allah we have put our trust.’ ‘Our Lord! Judge justly between us and our people, and You are the best of judges!’ (89)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتُمْ شُعَيْبًا إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ (٩٠)
```

```english
The elite of his people who were faithless said, ‘If you follow Shu'ayb, you will indeed be losers.’ (90)
```

```arabic
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٩١)
```

```english
So the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes. (91)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَا ۚ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٩٢)
```

```english
Those who impugned Shu'ayb became as if they had never lived there. Those who impugned Shu‘ayb were themselves the losers. (92)
```

```arabic
فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَنَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ ءَاسَىٰ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ (٩٣)
```

```english
So he abandoned them [to their fate] and said, ‘O my people! Certainly, I communicated to you the messages of my Lord, and I was your well-wisher. So how should I grieve for a faithless lot?’ (93)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِلى مَدْيَنَ أَخاهُمْ شُعَيْباً** — *And to Midian [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother.* (7:85) This verse is coordinated with the first story, which is the story of Nuh (peace be upon him). He (peace be upon him) built his call upon the foundation of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — just as the messengers mentioned in the preceding stories built theirs before him.

His saying: **قَدْ جَاءَتْكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ** — *There has already come to you a manifest proof from your Lord.* (7:85) indicates that he came with a sign (آية) proving his messengership (الرسالة). However, Allah, the Glorified, did not mention that specific sign in His Book. This sign is not the sign of punishment (آية العذاب) which Allah mentions at the end of his story, for the masses of his people among the disbelievers (الكفار) did not benefit from it; rather, it was their destruction. There is no meaning in the sign of punishment being a sign of messengership that clarifies the call.

Furthermore, He branches His saying: **فَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ وَالْمِيزَانَ** — *So observe fully the measure and the balance* (7:85) upon the coming of the sign, apparently. The call to act according to the religion (الدين) is only sound before the descent of punishment and the occurrence of destruction. This is evident.

He first called them—after monotheism, which is the root of the religion—to fulfill the measure and the balance and that they should **لا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْيَاءَهُمْ** — *not cheat the people of their goods* (7:85). Corruption (الإفساد) in transactions was prevalent and widespread among them.

Then he called them a second time with his saying: **وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَاحِهَا** — *and do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration* (7:85). This was a call to refrain from corruption on the earth after Allah had rectified it according to its nature and the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — which calls for its rectification so that the affairs of a happy life may be organized. Although "corruption on the earth" (الإفساد في الأرض), in its absolute sense, includes all acts of disobedience and sins—whether related to the rights of Allah or the rights of people—its juxtaposition with what precedes and follows it restricts it, approximately, to corruption that strips away public security regarding property, honor, and lives, such as highway robbery, plundering wealth, violating honor, and killing protected souls.

Then he justified his call to these two matters by saying: **ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ** — *That is better for you, should you be faithful.* (7:85). As for fulfilling the measure and balance and not cheating people of their goods being "better," it is because human social life, in its uprightness, is built upon exchange between individuals—each giving what exceeds their need and taking its equivalent to fulfill their deficiency in the necessities of life and its appendages. This requires public security in transactions whereby the descriptions and quantities of things are preserved as they are. Whoever permits himself to cheat people's goods permits the same for everyone like him, leading to its prevalence. If cheating, fraud, and risk (الغرر) become widespread—where one is not safe from poison replacing medicine, the inferior replacing the superior, or the adulterated replacing the pure, and ultimately anything replacing anything through various ruses and treatments—it would result in the destruction of both wealth and lives.

As for refraining from corrupting the earth being "better" for them, it is because the loss of public security halts the wheel of human society from moving in all directions, and in that lies the destruction of crops and progeny and the annihilation of humanity.

So the meaning is: Fulfilling the measure and balance, avoiding cheating, and refraining from corruption on the earth is better for you; its goodness will become apparent to you if you are truthful in my word and believe in me. Or the meaning is: That is better for you—you will know it is better if you are possessors of faith in the truth.

It has been said that the meaning is: "That is better for you if you believe in my call," for the non-believer does not benefit from these worldly goods in reality due to the disbelief (الكفر) they possess, which decrees their wretchedness, loss, and the straying of their efforts. This is because their benefit is only in an imaginary realm, which is the worldly life (الحياة الدنيا) that is but play, while **وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ** — *and the abode of the Hereafter is indeed the [true] Life, had they known.* (29:64).

All of this is on the assumption that the demonstrative pronoun in His saying **ذَٰلِكُمْ** (That) refers to fulfilling the measure and what follows it, as is the apparent context (السياق). As for taking the reference to include all that preceded and making the intended "faith" (الإيمان) the technical term rather than the linguistic meaning—as some have suggested—it is more akin to conditioning a thing upon itself. This is because the meaning would revert to something like: "If you are believers, then worshipping Allah alone by believing in Him, fulfilling the measure and balance, and not corrupting the earth is better for you."

A rebuttal to both of these latter views is that the appearance of His saying **إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ** (if you are/were believers) implies the established description of them having faith prior to the time of the address. This is necessitated by suspending the judgment on the phrase **كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ**, composed of the past tense of the deficient verb "to be" (كان) and the active participle (اسم الفاعل) of "faith," which implies the stability of the attribute in them over time. Such a meaning is not addressed to a people among whom are disbelievers, believers, the arrogant, and the submissive. Had it been as they say, the proper phrasing would have been: "That is better for you if you believe (إن آمنتم)" or "that you believe (أن تؤمنوا)." Thus, it appears there is no escape from the intended "faith" being something other than the technical religious term.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَا تَقْعُدُوا بِكُلِّ صِرَاطٍ تُوعِدُونَ وَتَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ مَنْ آمَنَ بِهِ وَتَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجاً** — *And do not lie in wait on every path to threaten and bar from the way of Allah those who have faith in Him, seeking to make it crooked.* (7:86). The apparent context is that **تُوعِدُونَ** (threatening) and **تَصُدُّونَ** (barring) are circumstantial qualifiers (حالان) for the subject of **لَا تَقْعُدُوا** (do not sit/lie in wait), and His saying **وَتَبْغُونَهَا** (seeking it [to be crooked]) is a circumstantial qualifier for the subject of **تَصُدُّونَ**.

Then he called them a third time to cease interfering with the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — which is the religion. The speech alludes to the fact that they used to sit on the path of those who believed in Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him), threatening them for their faith in him, their presence with him, their listening to him, and their performing religious acts of worship with him. They would turn them away from practicing the true religion and traveling the path of monotheism, while they themselves traveled the path of polytheism, seeking to make the way of Allah—which is the religion of the primordial nature (دين الفطرة)—crooked.

In short, they were cutting off the path to faith with all the power and trickery they could muster. So he forbade them from that and counseled them to remember Allah's favor upon them and to take heed by looking at what they knew of the history of past nations and the evil end (عاقبة السوء) that the corrupters (المفسدين) met.

Thus, His saying: **وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ كُنتُمْ قَلِيلاً فَكَثَّرَكُمْ وَانظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُفْسِدِينَ** — *And remember when you were few, and He multiplied you, and observe how was the fate of the agents of corruption.* (7:86) is speech cast in the mold of exhortation and counsel. it is capable of being connected to all the preceding commands and prohibitions.

His saying: **وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ كُنتُمْ قَلِيلاً فَكَثَّرَكُمْ** — *And remember when you were few, and He multiplied you* — is a command to remember their progression from fewness to many through the increase of progeny. This is among the great favors of Allah upon this human species, for a human being cannot live alone without society. The noble goal and high human happiness that distinguishes him from other animal species and others necessitated that Divine Providence grant him various tools, powers, and a specific existential composition. He cannot fulfill the necessities of his wondrous and diverse needs alone, but rather through cooperation with others in obtaining food, drink, clothing, shelter, marriage, and other things—a cooperation in thought, will, and action.

It is well known that whenever the number of those gathered increases, the composite social power increases, becoming stronger in its thought, will, and action. Thus, it perceives and senses the subtleties of needs and becomes alert to the fine ruses for subjugating natural forces to remove its deficiencies.

It is from the Divine favors that the human race is always increasing, progressing from fewness to many. This is one of the pillars in the species' journey from deficiency to perfection. Great nations are not like small bands that are snatched away from every side, nor are large peoples and tribes like small groups that are not independent in any of their political, economic, or military affairs, or other matters weighed by the scale of knowledge, will, and action.

As for the "fate of the agents of corruption" (عاقبة المفسدين), it is sufficient for insight to consider what has been narrated regarding the ends of the dominant, arrogant, and tyrannical nations that filled hearts with terror and souls with awe, destroyed lands, plundered wealth, shed blood, annihilated crowds, enslaved servants, and humiliated necks.

Allah gave them respite in their insolence and transgression until, when they reached the peak of their power and sat upon the throne of their might, the world deceived them with its adornment, and desires attracted them to their dissolution. This distracted them from the virtue of intellect (التعقل), and they occupied themselves with the amusements of life and living, taking their desires as their god. Allah led them astray based on knowledge, so they were stripped of power and will, deprived of favor, and scattered like the people of Saba (أيادي سبإ).

How many names of Caesars, Pharaohs, Chosroes, Emperors, and others are mentioned in the annals of time, of whom nothing remains but names—if they are not forgotten—and nothing is established of their hegemony but tales. It is from the Divine Sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — *the habitual way of Allah's action* — flowing in the cosmos that human life be built upon intellect. If it exceeds that and begins in corruption and causing corruption, the nature of the cosmos rejects it, the causes oppose it with their forces, grind it down with their masses, and strike it with every humiliation and wretchedness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِن كَانَ طَائِفَةٌ مِّنكُمْ آمَنُوا بِالَّذِي أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ** — *And if a group of you have believed in what I have been sent with...* (7:87) to the end of the verse. Then he called them a fourth time to patience (الصبر) on the assumption that disagreement would occur between them regarding faith and disbelief. He had been counseling them all, before this counsel, to unite upon faith in Allah and righteous deeds (الصالحات). It is as if he sensed from them that this would never happen, that disagreement was inevitable, and that the arrogant elite (الملأ المستكبرين) of his people—those who were threatening and barring from the way of Allah—would begin corrupting the earth and harming the believers. This would cause a weakening of resolve in the believers and allow people to dominate their hearts. So he commanded them all to be patient and wait for Allah's command regarding them, that He might judge between them, and He is the best of judges.

In this lies the rectification of society. As for the believers, they do not fall into despair regarding a secure life, nor into agitation and confusion regarding their religion. As for the disbelievers, they do not fall into the regret of proceeding without foresight or the corruption of wrongdoing out of ignorance. Allah's judgment is the best decider between the two groups, for He is the best of judges; He does not slacken in judgment when its time arrives, nor does He act unjustly in judgment when He judges.

Thus, his saying: **فَاصْبِرُوا** — *so be patient* — is a directive command (أمر إرشادي) with respect to the disbelievers, and a masterly or directive command with respect to the believers. It is a direction to all toward what rectifies their state.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ الْمَلأُ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا مِن قَوْمِهِ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يَا شُعَيْبُ** — *The elite of his people who were arrogant said, ‘O Shu‘ayb, we will surely expel you...’* (7:88). The arrogant elite of his people did not take guidance from what he directed them toward regarding patience and waiting for the decisive judgment from Allah, the Glorified. Instead, they rushed to threaten him and the believers with expulsion from their land unless they returned to their creed (ملتهم) by apostatizing from the religion of monotheism.

In their emphasizing the statement **لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ** (we will surely expel you) and **لَتَعُودُنَّ** (you shall surely return) with an oath and the emphatic *nun* (نون التأكيد), there is an indication of their firm resolve to do so. Therefore, he (peace be upon him) rushed, after hearing this statement from them, to seek victory and an opening from Allah, the Glorified.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَارِهِينَ قَدِ افْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً إِنْ عُدْنَا فِي مِلَّتِكُم** — *He said, ‘What! Even if we should be unwilling? We would be fabricating a lie against Allah should we return to your creed...’* (7:88-89). He (peace be upon him) replied by expressing unwillingness to return to their creed, as evidenced by the subsequent sentences. The necessary consequence of this is choosing the other alternative in the event of being forced into one of them, as they had informed him.

He (peace be upon him) answered on behalf of himself and the believers from his people, mentioning that he and the believers are all unwilling to return to their creed. For in that would be the fabrication of a lie against Allah, the Glorified, by attributing partners to Him, and the fabricated rulings that follow in the religion of idolatry. Thus, his saying: **قَدِ افْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً** — *We would be fabricating a lie against Allah* — serves as the justification for his saying: **أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَارِهِينَ** — *Even if we should be unwilling?*

Among the most absurd arguments is using his saying: **إِنْ عُدْنَا فِي مِلَّتِكُم بَعْدَ إِذْ نَجَّانَا اللَّهُ مِنْهَا** — *should we return to your creed after Allah has delivered us from it* — as evidence that Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) was a polytheist idolater before his prophethood—far be it from him! It was just mentioned that he speaks for himself and for the believers from his people, and they were indeed disbelieving polytheists before believing in him. So Allah delivered them from the creed of polytheism and guided them through Shu‘ayb to monotheism. Thus, Shu‘ayb's saying: **نَجَّانَا اللَّهُ** — *Allah has delivered us* — is speaking on behalf of the group by attributing the description of the majority to the whole. This is if "deliverance" (التنجية) refers to the outward deliverance from actual polytheism. However, if it refers to true deliverance—which is the removal from every actual or potential and expected error—then Shu‘ayb (who did not associate partners with Allah for the blink of an eye) and his people (who were polytheists before the time of their faith in Shu‘ayb) are all among those whom Allah delivered from polytheism. For a human being possesses for his own perishing self neither harm nor benefit, and whatever good reaches him is from Allah, the Glorified.

His saying: **وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَا أَن نَّعُودَ فِيهَا إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ رَبُّنَا** — *It does not behove us to return to it, unless Allah, our Lord, should wish.* (7:89) is like a transition (إضراب) and an escalation (ترقي) to a definitive answer. It is as if he said: "We are unwilling to return to your creed because it involves fabrication against Allah; rather, that is something that shall never be." This is because the unwillingness toward something only makes engaging in it difficult, not impossible. So he (peace be upon him) answered secondly with the impossibility of returning after his first answer regarding its difficulty, which is the transition and escalation we mentioned.

Since his saying: **وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَا أَن نَّعُودَ فِيهَا** (It does not behove us to return to it) is in the sense of saying: "We shall never return to it," and making a definitive claim in such resolves is far from the etiquette of prophethood—for it would mean: "We shall not return under any assumed condition, even if Allah willed," which is ignorance of His station, the Exalted—he excepted the will of Allah, the Glorified, saying: **إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ رَبُّنَا** — *unless Allah, our Lord, should wish.* For a human being, however they may be, is liable to error. It is possible that one might err through a sin, and Allah might punish him by withdrawing His care from him, thereby expelling him from His religion so that he perishes in misguidance.

In the combination of the two names in His saying: **اللَّهُ رَبُّنَا** (Allah, our Lord), there is an indication that Allah, who decrees what He wills, is the One who manages our affairs; He is both God (إله) and Lord (رب), as necessitated by the religion of monotheism. This is not as the religion of idolatry teaches, for it concedes Divinity (الألوهية) to Allah but then separates Lordship (الربوبية) with its various functions among idols, calling them "Lord of the Sea," "Lord of the Land," and so on.

His saying: **وَسِعَ رَبُّنَا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً** — *Our Lord embraces all things in [His] knowledge.* (7:89) is like a justification for following the speech with the exception. It is as if it were asked: "Why did you make an exception after you spoke absolutely and were definitive in resolve?" He replied: "Because my Lord embraces all things in knowledge, and I do not encompass anything of His knowledge except what He wills. It is possible that His will might pertain to something hidden from my knowledge—whether it displeases or pleases me—such as His knowledge pertaining to the fact that we will disobey Him in some of His commands, and thus He wills our return to your creed, even if we are unwilling today." Perhaps this meaning is the reason for following this statement with something like: **عَلَى اللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا** — *In Allah we have put our trust.* (7:89). For whoever puts their trust in Allah, He is sufficient for them and protects them from the evil of what they fear.

When the speech reached this level of eloquence—and they had informed them of their resolve on one of two matters: expulsion or return, and Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) had informed them of the definitive resolve never to return to their creed—he (peace be upon him) turned to his Lord and sought an opening, saying on behalf of himself and the believers: **رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ الْفَاتِحِينَ** — *Our Lord! Judge between us and our people with justice, and You are the best of judges.* (7:89). He asks his Lord to "open" (يفتح) between them—that is, between Shu‘ayb and the believers, and the polytheists of his people—which is the decisive judgment. For an "opening" (الفتح) between two things necessitates distancing each from the other so that this does not touch that, nor that this. He (peace be upon him) prayed for the "opening" and used it as a metaphor for the decisive judgment, which is destruction or its equivalent. He left ambiguous who would be the loser and who the winner, who the perished and who the saved, while he knew that Allah would surely grant him victory and that disgrace and evil this day are upon the disbelievers. However, he (peace be upon him) adopted fairness for the sake of truth and observed etiquette by returning the matter in that regard to Allah, just as he did in his previous saying: **فَاصْبِرُوا حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ اللَّهُ بَيْنَنَا وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْحَاكِمِينَ** — *so be patient until Allah judges between us, and He is the best of judges.* (7:87).

"The Best of Judges" (خير الحاكمين) and "The Best of Deciders/Openers" (خير الفاتحين) are two of the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) of Allah. The discussion on the meaning of "judgment" (الحكم) has preceded, and the meaning of "opening" (الفتح) was just mentioned. A comprehensive discussion on the Beautiful Names will come in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا** — *To Allah belong the Best Names, so supplicate Him by them* (7:180) in this Surah, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقَالَ الْمَلأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِن قَوْمِهِ** — *The faithless elite of his people said...* (7:90) to the end of the verse. This is a threat from them to whoever believed in Shu‘ayb or intended to believe in him. It is part of the threatening and barring that Shu‘ayb was forbidding with his saying: **وَلَا تَقْعُدُوا بِكُلِّ صِرَاطٍ تُوعِدُونَ وَتَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ**. The singling out of this statement here among all their other sayings is to serve as a preamble and preparation for what will come of their saying after the mention of their destruction: **الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كَانُوا هُمُ الْخَاسِرِينَ** — *Those who impugned Shu‘ayb became the losers.* (7:92).

It is possible that "following" (الاتباع) is meant in its apparent conventional sense, which is following the footsteps of one walking on a path. This would be because the arrogant elite, having forced him and those with him into one of two matters—leaving their land or returning to their creed—and then hearing him reject the return to their creed with a definitive rejection and then supplicate with his saying: **رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِالْحَقِّ**, did not doubt that he would leave them and migrate to a land other than theirs, and that the believers from the people would follow him in this migration. At that point, they addressed the group of believers, saying: **لَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتُمْ شُعَيْباً إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً لَّخَاسِرُونَ** — *If you follow Shu‘ayb, you will indeed be losers.* (7:90). They threatened them and frightened them with loss if they followed him in leaving their land, so that Shu‘ayb would leave alone. For they were primarily hostile toward him, while the believers were only hated because of him and for his sake.

Whichever of the two views it may be, the verse is like a preamble and preparation for the coming verse: **الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كَانُوا هُمُ الْخَاسِرِينَ**, as previously indicated.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دَارِهِمْ جَاثِمِينَ** — *So the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless in their homes.* (7:91). **أَصْبَحُوا** means they became or entered into the morning. The meaning of the verse has preceded in its counterpart in the story of Salih.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيهَا** — *Those who impugned Shu‘ayb, [it was] as if they had never lived there...* (7:92) to His saying: **الْخَاسِرِينَ** (the losers). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "One *ghaniya* (غني) in such a place when his stay there was long, being independent (مستغنياً) of others by means of it." He said: **كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيهَا** (end quote). And **كَأَن** is the lightened form of *ka'anna* (كأنّ), lightened because of its entry into a verbal sentence.

So His saying: **الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيهَا** contains a comparison of the state of the beliers among his people to those who did not stay long in their land. Such people are easily removed because they have no attachment to it in terms of clan, family, home, estates, or property. As for one who has established himself in a land, settled it, stayed long in it, and attached himself to it with everything that one attaches to in material life, leaving it is difficult for him, almost impossible—especially the leaving of a nation residing in its land and what it has acquired therein throughout its long stay. Yet these people—and they were a nation deeply rooted in the land—left their home and what was in it in the shortest time; the earthquake seized them, and they became lifeless in their homes.

They used to claim that Shu‘ayb and those who followed him would be the losers, but their assumption failed, and the tables were turned upon them, so they were the losers. They plotted, and Allah plotted, and Allah is the best of plotters.

To this Allah points where He first mentions their saying that the followers of Shu‘ayb are losers, then He mentions the descent of the punishment and leaves ambiguous those whom the earthquake seized, saying: **فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ** — *So the earthquake seized them* — and He did not say: "So the earthquake seized those who disbelieved." Then He explicitly stated in His saying: **الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً** — *Those who impugned Shu‘ayb* — that the Divine judgment, destruction, and loss were for those who belied him from his people, rather than for Shu‘ayb and those who followed him. Thus, they were the losers, the ones against whom the plot was turned, while they had assumed otherwise.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ** — *So he left them...* (7:93) to the end of the verse. The apparent context is that he only turned away after the descent of the punishment upon them and their destruction, and that the address is an address of taking heed. His saying: **فَكَيْفَ آسَىٰ** — *so how should I grieve* — is from *al-asa* (الأسى), meaning "how should I be sad." The rest is clear.
